question: Explain the purpose of maintaining CNV vacuum.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core using natural circulation and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational control, including degasification lines and emergency core cooling. Its pressure-retaining parts help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, preventing the release of radionuclides.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using installed instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits set in plant technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and design requirements for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for corrosion resistance and compliance with ASME BPVC standards. It also outlines the preservice and inservice inspection and testing procedures for pressure-retaining components, specifying adherence to the latest ASME BPVC Section XI requirements as mandated by 10 CFR 50.55a regulations.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with NRC regulations and ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) requirements for overpressure protection of the reactor coolant boundary. It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and applied, ensuring they meet the conditions specified in relevant NRC regulatory guides and federal regulations. A consolidated list of the specific ASME code cases used is provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components in the NuScale reactor, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility with operating environments. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the applicable material specifications for components such as the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, and bolting.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the methodology and regulatory compliance for establishing pressure-temperature operational limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It details the use of ASME and NRC guidelines to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and specifies that these limits are designed to prevent non-ductile failure of the reactor pressure vessel.

edges.description: Section A describes how the RCS degasification and venting to the CNV help remove noncondensible gases, which is relevant to Section B's discussion of leakage detection inside containment, as maintaining CNV vacuum aids in identifying and managing potential RCPB leakage.

Section A describes the system's mechanisms for removing noncondensible gases from the PZR and maintaining RCS integrity, while Section B details the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners that help ensure the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, thus both sections are connected through their focus on maintaining the integrity and safety of the RCS and its pressure boundary.

Section A describes how the CNV vacuum is maintained to remove noncondensible gases from the reactor system, which supports Section B's focus on maintaining the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary as required by regulatory codes.

Section A discusses the challenges of detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) leakage, while Section B specifies the material and inspection requirements for threaded fasteners that are part of the RCPB, connecting them through their shared focus on maintaining RCPB integrity to prevent leakage.

Section A discusses the challenges of detecting reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, which is relevant to Section B's focus on compliance with overpressure protection codes, as effective leakage detection (potentially aided by maintaining CNV vacuum) supports the integrity and regulatory compliance of the pressure boundary.

Section A describes the material and code compliance for threaded fasteners in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B outlines the use of ASME BPVC code cases and regulatory compliance for the design and construction of the same boundary, connecting them through their shared reliance on ASME BPVC standards for ensuring boundary integrity.

Section A references the material composition and structural components of the pressurizer, while Section B describes the system function of removing noncondensible gases from the pressurizer volume, connecting them through the operational context of the pressurizer's role in maintaining system integrity and vacuum.

Section A describes the structural components and materials of the pressurizer, which is part of the reactor coolant system, while Section B discusses the detection of leakage from the reactor coolant pressure boundary, with both sections connected by their focus on the integrity and monitoring of the reactor coolant system and its components.

Section A describes the components and materials of the pressurizer, while Section B details the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in those components, connecting them through the shared focus on material selection and integrity for reactor coolant system pressure boundary components.

Section B explains that the CNV (containment vessel) receives noncondensible gases vented from the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) during both normal and emergency operations, which relates to the purpose of maintaining CNV vacuum by ensuring these gases are effectively removed to maintain system integrity.

Section B discusses the detection of reactor coolant pressure boundary leakage, which is directly related to the purpose of maintaining CNV vacuum as a means to monitor and identify such leakage, while Section A provides structural and examination details that ensure the integrity of components relevant to leakage pathways.

Section A discusses compliance of lock plate stud welds with ASME BPVC requirements, while Section B details the material selection and ASME BPVC compliance of threaded fasteners in the reactor pressure vessel, connecting them through their shared adherence to ASME code requirements for reactor vessel components.
